resume:
PRIYA NAIR
Marketing Intern Candidate | Rising Senior, B.S. Marketing | priya.nair@email.com

SUMMARY
Data-curious marketing student focused on growth and content. Comfortable with analytics, copywriting, and social. Looking for a summer growth-marketing internship.

EXPERIENCE

GreenLeaf Coffee Co. (local roaster) — Marketing Intern
Summer 2024 (June–August)
- Ran the brand's Instagram and TikTok, growing combined followers from 4,200 to 11,000.
- Wrote and scheduled all organic social content; posted 4–5x per week.
- Launched the brand's first email newsletter; grew the list to 1,800 subscribers.
- Note elsewhere on resume lists this same role as 'May–July 2024.'

Campus Eats (student food-delivery startup) — Growth Marketing Lead (volunteer)
Spring 2024
- 'Led a team of six' on a referral campaign that drove 900 new student signups in one semester.
- Designed the referral incentive structure and the landing page.
- (Founder bio on the Campus Eats site lists Priya as 'one of two marketing volunteers.')

University Marketing Club — Social Media Chair
2023–2024
- Managed the club's LinkedIn and Instagram.
- Grew event attendance by 'over 200%' one semester; a separate section of this resume states event attendance 'roughly doubled.'

SKILLS
Google Analytics 4, Meta Ads Manager, Canva, Mailchimp, basic SQL, A/B testing.
Claims both 'fluent in SQL' under Skills and 'basic SQL exposure' under Summary.

EDUCATION
B.S. Marketing, expected 2026. GPA listed as 3.8 in the header and 3.6 in the education section.

NOTES
- Available full-time June–August.
- Resume header says 'rising senior'; education line implies graduation 2026, consistent with rising junior in some sections.
jd:
Summer Growth Marketing Intern — T89 (consumer mobile app, Series A)

We are a consumer habit-tracking app hiring a summer growth-marketing intern to support paid social, lifecycle email, and organic content experiments. You will work alongside a growth marketer and a content lead. This is a learning role, but we want someone who is precise about what they actually did versus what a team did, who can read a basic funnel, and who is honest about the limits of their experience.

What we look for in an intern:
- Genuine hands-on ownership of at least one channel or campaign, described specifically.
- The ability to separate personal contribution from team results.
- Comfort with basic analytics (GA4 or similar) and a willingness to say 'I don't know that yet.'
- Clear, honest communication — we read resumes closely and will ask about anything that doesn't line up.

Reports to the Head of Growth. 12-week paid internship, in-person in Austin or remote.

history.q: Hi Priya, thanks for coming in. Tell me briefly which marketing experience you'd point to as most hands-on.
At GreenLeaf, you grew combined social from 4,200 to 11,000. Over exactly what window did that happen?
I see the role dated two different ways on your resume — June to August, and May to July. Which is correct?
On the email newsletter — was that genuinely the brand's first, and did you build it solo?
What was the open rate on those newsletters, roughly, and how did you measure it?
Let's go to Campus Eats. Your resume says you 'led a team of six.' Walk me through who was on that team.
The Campus Eats site describes you as one of two marketing volunteers. Help me reconcile that with 'led a team of six.'
The referral campaign drove 900 signups. What part of that 900 do you personally attribute to your incentive design?
For the Marketing Club, attendance is listed as 'over 200%' in one place and 'roughly doubled' in another. Which is it?
Your skills list says 'fluent in SQL' but your summary says 'basic SQL exposure.' Where are you really?
If I gave you a GA4 dashboard and asked for the signup conversion rate by channel, could you pull it?
What's a marketing tool or skill on this job description you genuinely don't have yet?
Tell me about a campaign of yours that didn't work, and what you learned.
Last thing — your GPA shows as 3.8 in the header and 3.6 in the education section. Which is accurate, and what happened there?

candidate_last_answer: The 3.6 is accurate. The 3.8 is a leftover from an earlier version of this resume — it was my GPA after sophomore year, but it dropped to 3.6 after a hard analytics course last fall, and I forgot to update the header. That's on me, and I should have caught it before sending. While we're on it, I'd flag two other things on this resume I'd correct: the Campus Eats line should say 'one of two volunteers,' not 'led a team of six' — I designed the referral incentive and the landing page, but I didn't lead anyone. And GreenLeaf was June to August; the May to July date is wrong.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I'd flag two other things on this resume I'd correct

expected-yield: test whether her proactive self-correction is genuine self-awareness or selective damage control — have her name what process she'll use to prevent resume inconsistencies going forward, and whether any remaining claim (the 11,000 followers, the 1,800 subscribers, the 'over 200%' attendance) still overstates her solo contribution
